column20:
No, wine grape cultivation does not dominate land use in Chile, since vineyards represent less than 50% of the agricultural area and only about 15% of agricultural production units nationwide (UPA, 2007). Nonetheless, it is one of the most dynamic and strategic sectors of Chilean agriculture. The industry covers around 114,000 hectares, concentrated mainly in the Maule (43%) and O’Higgins (28%) regions, and although smaller in land footprint compared to crops and livestock, its productivity, export orientation, and employment generation are disproportionately high.
 
 Wine contributes 0.5% of Chile’s GDP, generates more than 100,000 direct jobs (53% in vineyards), and supports 800 wineries, 11,697 producers, and 394 exporting companies, 76% of which are SMEs. In addition, wine exports represent 5.7% of non-copper exports, 16.5% of agricultural exports, and US$205 million in tax revenues. The sector has specialized in high-quality varieties adapted to regional conditions, with Denomination of Origin wines comprising 85% of production.
 
 In summary, while vineyards do not dominate land use in Chile, they are a leading agricultural sector in economic relevance, export value, and rural employment, sustaining regional economies particularly in Maule and O’Higgins.
column21: Direct evidence of pest suppression by bats (e.g. exclusion studies, reductions in pest damage where bats are present).
column22:
Current evidence from central Chile demonstrates that insectivorous bats contribute significantly to pest suppression in vineyards. Experimental exclosure studies provide the strongest evidence: Rodríguez-San Pedro et al. (2020) showed that excluding bats from vines increased herbivory rates and grape cluster damage, with control plots experiencing 7% less damage and economic benefits estimated at US$188–248/ha/year due to bat predation. This confirms a direct top-down regulatory effect of bats on grapevine pest insects.
 
 Complementary studies based on acoustic monitoring (Rodríguez-San Pedro et al. 2019, 2021; Puelles-Escobar & Muñoz-Sáez 2024) reveal that bat activity and feeding are consistently higher at vineyard edges, especially adjacent to native vegetation. Key species such as Tadarida brasiliensis, Myotis chiloensis, and Lasiurus varius increase their foraging activity in structurally diverse landscapes, indicating that landscape heterogeneity and habitat conservation amplify the pest control service.
 
 In summary, there is robust experimental and observational evidence that bats suppress insect pests in Chilean vineyards, with measurable reductions in crop damage and associated economic benefits. Maintaining vineyard edges, hedgerows, and native vegetation remnants enhances bat-mediated pest control, reinforcing their role as a key component of agroecological vineyard management.

column25:
The current understanding of bat pest suppression services in Chilean vineyards is best categorized as option 2: From a few sites or seasons. Experimental evidence comes from a bat-exclosure study in three vineyards of the Maipo Valley, conducted over 12 weeks in the 2017–2018 growing season, which demonstrated that vines without bat access experienced significantly higher leaf herbivory and grape cluster damage, with estimated grower savings of up to 7% of production due to bat predation (Rodríguez-San Pedro et al., 2020).
 
 Subsequent acoustic monitoring campaigns expanded the spatial scale to 16 vineyards in central Chile, with systematic surveys using 64 ultrasonic detectors and repeated sampling across two crop seasons. These studies showed that bat activity is consistently higher at vineyard edges, especially when adjacent to native vegetation, and identified Tadarida brasiliensis, Myotis chiloensis, and Lasiurus varius as key species contributing to ecosystem services (Rodríguez-San Pedro et al., 2019; Rodríguez-San Pedro et al., 2021).
 
 Most recently, a large-scale study deployed 48 AudioMoths during summer 2022–2023 and 56 during spring 2023–2024 across 11 vineyards and adjacent habitats. Each device recorded nightly over ~28 days per season, totaling 2,601 recorder-nights across vineyard interiors, edges, and native vegetation. Results confirmed that habitat heterogeneity enhances bat richness and feeding activity, reinforcing the importance of maintaining native vegetation and vineyard edges for agroecological management (Puelles-Escobar & Muñoz-Sáez, 2024).
 
 Despite these significant contributions, the available evidence is still temporally limited (≤2 years of monitoring) and geographically concentrated in the Mediterranean valleys of central Chile. Broader replication across different viticultural valleys and over longer time frames is necessary.

There are evidences that insectivorous bats roosting both in rural villages and inside protected areas consume pests and disease vectors in these regions
 Madagascar:
 *Tuneu-Corral et al. 2025. Beyond borders: The role of protected areas in promoting bat-mediated pest suppression in rural areas of Madagascar (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167880925001227)
  - Up to 55 crop pests consumed by insectivorous bats (including 11 rice pests)
  - Up to 15 disease vector species consumed by insectivorous bats roosting in protected areas and in synanthropic roosts
 
 *Kemp et al. 2019. Bats as potential suppressors of multiple agricultural pests: A case study from Madagascar. (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167880918303761)
  - Up to 6 known pest species, seven insect vectors of human diseases and 17 potential pest taxa consumed by synanthropic bat species.

column22:
We have published a systematic review and several papers describing studies developed in Spain.
 
A global systematic review on bat ecosystem services in agricultural landscapes:
 *Tuneu-Corral et al. 2023. Pest suppression by bats and management strategies to favour it: a global review. (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/brv.12967)

 Spain:
 * Tuneu-Corral et al. 2024. Bats and rice: Quantifying the role of insectivorous bats as agricultural pest suppressors in rice fields. (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212041624000093)
  - Using exclosures in rice fields, we observed a reduction of over 50% in crop damage caused by pests in the presence of bats. We quantified the ecosystem services provided by bats at an average value of €56 per hectare in our study area, which extrapolates to an average of approximately €6 million at the national level.
 
 *Tuneu-Corral et al. 2025. Landscape of fear in rice paddies: effect of bat ultrasounds on the egg production of a tympanate moth pest. (https://scijournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/ps.8866)
  - We studied the effect of bat ultrasounds on the egg production of the striped rice stem borer moth (a tympanate species considered a major rice pest) using captivity experiments and acoustic recordings. In the presence of bat ultrasounds, the number of eggs per clutch was reduced by half. This suggests that bats may protect rice not only through direct predation, but also by merely emitting ultrasounds into the environment.
 
 *Puig-Montserrat et al. 2015. Pest control service provided by bats in Mediterranean rice paddies: linking agroecosystems structure to ecological functions. (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1016/j.mambio.2015.03.008)
 
 *Puig-Montserrat et al. 2020. Bats actively prey on mosquitoes and other deleterious insects in rice paddies: Potential impact on human health and agriculture. (https://scijournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/ps.5925)
 
 Another study carried out in olive plantation in Spain:
 *Puig-Montserrat et al. 2021. Benefits of organic olive farming for the conservation of gleaning bats. (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167880921000657)

column22: High foraging and feeding activity of at least 8 species of insectivorous bats ((Mammalian Biology: 100:73-80)), correlation between pest and bat foraging activity. The diet of Pipistrellus kuhlii was evaluated using meta-barcoding. This is the most common insectivorous bat in the country and the most common species in agricultural areas in the north and center areas of the country. 27 species of agricultural pests were found in the diet, including pests of key economic concern such as the pink bollworm, Pectinophora gossypiella. There was a significant correlation between the abundance of the Pink bollworm with its occurrence in the diet of the bats. Evidence exists that the bats exploits Pink bollworm irruptions by opportunistic feeding (Molecular Ecology, 29:1185–1198). Exclusion studies are conducted.

column22:
This is a hard question to answer - there are lots of knowledge gaps overall about the bats (and the broader ecology) in this system even at the most basic level - from their presence/absence, to their actual taxonomy (new undescribed species in the system). Most of these species do not have quantified or qualitative descriptions of their diet. This is true across a lot of Western Africa in the areas of the Upper Guinea rainforest.
 
 What we know is from trapping, collecting faecal samples, metabarcoding and dietary analysis. another challenge of this is the low level of reference genetic material with which to compare our dietary items. You can only get less than 10% of the insects to species/genus. A large amount can only be identified to Order
 
 The work is not published but it hopefully will start to be in about a year (working on a number of different papers for the system)

column37:
I think a common problem is lack of funding. Most projects get money to test some specific questions and afterwards researchers just move on to next study system. We need long term monitoring studies when tackling the role of bats as pest suppressors. But how to make it sexy to be able to get this funding?
When dealing with companies like Nestle we should aim to guarantee a constant funding to be able to track this pest suppression across seasons and years (let's say 10 years), and understand if propose measures work in the long term.

It is seasonally grown during the Indian summer-monsoon, in valleys with either other plantations or forests in the surroundings or in plains, with small human settlements in the surroundings. The minimum landholding size is about an acre and can go up to 15-20 acres, depending on landholders' capacities. Rest of the year, the land is usually left as fallow.
column21: Bats known to consume pests or overlap in activity with pests (indirect evidence of bat benefits).
column22: In my study region, my work is the only available study on rice-paddy pest control. At present this is a chapter in my doctoral thesis (finished in 2023) and yet to be published as a paper, which is in preparation. I used overnight acoustic recordings, manually counted total activity and feeding buzzes of bats, conducted some insect trapping, and compared field observations with available literature from the area on rice-paddy pests. The study had originally planned installation of exclosure experiments too, but could not be done due to the COVID pandemic. Benefits from bats for pest control were reported by local rice-growers in my interviews, and this was supported by high bat activity and feeding rates in the rice-fields (with and without pesticide use) I monitored across 2 seasons of rice-paddy growth.

column37: Studies on the ecology of insectivorous bats and their translation to ecosystem services such as insect pest control have been limited in India. So, we probably need to start right from building research capacity in bat ecology, to conduct systematic and robust studies on insect bat acoustics, foraging/ feeding ecology, handling and processing of large acoustic data, interdisciplinary approches in understanding the dynamics of these settings as socio-ecological systems, and up to quantification of pest control services by bats. General level of awareness and knowledge among the local farmers and authorities need to be improved for building local support to bat-focused management stretegies.

So, as mentioned for the paddy crop above, building research capacity in conducting systematic and robust field studies on the foraging ecology of the bat species, seasonality, predator-prey relationships, and interdisciplinary approches in understanding the dynamics of these socio-ecological systems will be critical. column17: The study was conducted in intensive agricultural landscapes dominated by large-scale arable fields with low proportions of semi-natural habitats. Farms represented three contrasting management systems: conventional farming, organic farming, and soil conservation farming. Landscapes were primarily composed of cereal and oilseed crops with scattered hedgerows, grassy margins and small woodland patches.
column19: The study system consisted of crop rotations typical of northern European arable farming. Rotations varied among farms but generally included cereals (wheat, barley), oilseed rape, maize and occasionally legumes or cover crops. Continuous monoculture was uncommon in the study area.
column21: Bats known to consume pests or overlap in activity with pests (indirect evidence of bat benefits).
column22: Our study identified positive trophic associations between phytophagous insect abundance and bat activity in intensive arable landscapes of northern France. Wildflower strips increased moth and phytophagous insect abundance, and these increases were associated with higher bat activity, particularly for mid-range echolocators. However, our study was correlative and did not directly measure pest suppression or economic benefits.

column27: Some empirical studies or surveys identify several key limiting factors in this specific system (e.g., roost loss, pesticides), but evidence is incomplete, inconsistent, or geographically limited.
column28: Within our study system, bat activity appeared associated with prey availability and landscape context. Trophic relationships between phytophagous insects and bats were mainly detected in landscapes with higher proportions of organic farming. In simplified landscapes, wildflower strips increased insect abundance strongly, but trophic links with bats were weaker or only marginally significant. These results suggest that landscape-scale agricultural management and habitat heterogeneity may influence bat activity and trophic interactions.

column31: Our study suggests that perennial wildflower strips can enhance nocturnal insect abundance and may indirectly benefit bats through increased prey availability. The results also indicate that the effectiveness of these measures depends on landscape context, particularly the proportion of organic farming and landscape heterogeneity. Combining flower strips with broader reductions in agricultural intensification may therefore improve outcomes for bats.

column25: The study included 448 hedgerows monitored between 2020 and 2023 across three French agricultural regions and multiple agricultural landscape contexts. column28: The study identified hedgerow trimming method, height and connectivity as the strongest predictors of bat activity. Pollarding and tall tree-filled hedgerows generally increased activity of short- and mid-range echolocators, while coppicing often reduced activity for clutter-adapted bats. Connected hedgerows also supported higher activity than dead-end hedgerows.

column22: There are many papers and evidence that the dominant species in this region (Tadarida brasiliensis) is an important pest suppressor of corn and grape pest, and about the economic benefits that they provide. However, that evidence comes mainly from the US and has not been regionally verified. In central-north Mexico (Zacatecas, Durango, Aguascalientes), there are no studies that show bats suppressing pests. We haven't done bat diet studies in the region due to financial constraints. With the knowledge of bats suppressing maize and grape pests, we are transferring that knowledge into our system.

column25:
The bat-friendly management strategies started more than 5 years ago, however, our understanding of bat pest suppression services is very limited because there is no funding to conduct diet analyses. 
 Regional pests are still unknown to us due to the lack of research in this area of Mexico. Our strength is the knowledge and time scale of one management strategy (bat boxes) but the basic knowledge of bat pest suppression is not known.

column28:
We have evidence of subterranean roosts mainly mines in the region to be highly disturbed, but also agricultural intensification and extensification is on the rise, habitat loss and lack of water sources. The last two are the main limiting factors (habitat loss and lack of water sources) as Zacatecas is a very arid region mainly with shrubland with a high degree of deforestation, fires and abandoned farmlands (no vegetation at all). 
 Noise, agrochemicals and light pollution is not a big problem as is a subsistence farming. However, there is a large wind farm nearby (around 200 turbines).
 The lack of water is such a big problem even for human needs.

column21: Direct evidence of pest suppression by bats (e.g. exclusion studies, reductions in pest damage where bats are present).
column22:
We first demonstrated the value of pest suppression service by dietary analysis (Taylor et al., 2018, Weier et al. 2019a) then exclusion of insectivorous birds and bats from exclosure plots - and assessing damage levels of two main pests, stinkbugs (Linden et al. 2019) and tortrid moths (Bourakia et al., 2023). Then repeated exclosure experiments across an elevation/climate gradient, incorporating flower exclusion treatments within predator exclusion plots to assess the interaction between pollination and pest predation (Anders et al. 2024). All experiments demonstrated the key role of remaining natural habitats, the proportion of which in a landscape was correlated with both pollination & predation services. We also showed that agricultural intensification impacted negatively on the occurrence of slow clutter bats like horseshoe bats (Weier et al. 2021). We have summarized many of our management recommendations in Weier et al. (2024).

column22: The study was conducted in Hungarian vineyards and provides moderate-to-strong evidence that bats contribute to biological pest control. Bat activity in spring was negatively associated with the abundance of the European grapevine moth (Lobesia botrana), one of the most important vineyard pests in Europe. Experimental exclusion of birds and bats increased fruit damage and reduced estimated yield, indicating that vertebrate predators provide economically relevant pest-control services. The study estimated that exclusion of birds and bats reduced grape yield by approximately 73 kg/ha on average. Evidence for bat-specific pest suppression is strengthened by the observed negative relationship between bat activity and L. botrana abundance, although the exclusion experiment did not separate bat and bird effects. The findings are consistent with previous European studies showing that bats frequently consume grapevine moths and can reduce vineyard pest populations.
column24: From a few sites or seasons - data collected from more than one farm or site, or during limited crop seasons (1 - 3 years). The representativeness of the findings to the whole system is limited.
column25: The evidence is moderately representative across space and time for Hungarian vineyard systems. The study included 12 vineyards spanning organic and integrated pest management (IPM) systems and contrasting forested and non-forested landscapes, providing coverage of major environmental gradients within the region. However, data were collected during a single year and within one wine-growing region of Hungary. Therefore, while the results are likely representative of similar Central European vineyard landscapes, caution is needed when extrapolating across years, climatic conditions, or substantially different vineyard systems. Confidence is strengthened by the fact that the observed patterns are consistent with other European studies reporting consumption of grapevine moths by bats and positive effects of bat activity on vineyard pest control.
column27: Strong empirical evidence identifies key limiting factors in this system with clear relevance to bat conservation or pest suppression.
column28: The level of understanding is moderate. In Hungarian vineyard landscapes, the strongest evidence points to landscape structure, particularly the availability and proximity of forests and other woody habitats, as important factors influencing bat activity. The study found significantly higher bat activity in spring in vineyards embedded in forested landscapes with greater connectivity of woody elements (forest patches, hedgerows, and tree groups). These habitats likely provide key foraging resources and roosting opportunities. Forested landscapes were also associated with lower fruit damage, suggesting that the positive effect of forests on bat activity may translate into enhanced pest-control services. In contrast, local vineyard management (organic versus IPM) had no detectable effect on bat activity. While forest cover and habitat connectivity appear to be important drivers, further research is needed to understand temporal variation, roost limitation, and the relative importance of different factors across regions and years.
column30: Several management strategies have been tried or tested in limited contexts in this system, showing promise but with incomplete validation (short-term trials, lack of control groups) or limited geographic application (local trials without broad replicates) or unclear relationship between the management strategies tested and the mechanisms.
column31: In Hungarian vineyard landscapes, the most promising bat-supportive management strategy is the conservation and restoration of nearby woody habitats. The study found higher bat activity in vineyards embedded in forested landscapes and demonstrated that increased bat activity was associated with lower abundance of the major vineyard pest, Lobesia botrana. Based on these findings, maintaining deciduous forest patches, hedgerows, tree lines, and other connected woody elements around vineyards is likely to enhance bat activity and associated pest-control services. The study also suggests that increasing landscape connectivity and retaining native woody vegetation can improve habitat quality for bats. While these recommendations are supported by empirical evidence from the region, their effectiveness has not yet been tested through large-scale management interventions or long-term monitoring programmes.

column34: No formal monitoring programme of bat-supportive management interventions has been reported for Hungarian vineyard systems. The available evidence comes primarily from observational and experimental ecological studies comparing landscapes that differ in forest cover and habitat connectivity. Although these studies identify management actions likely to benefit bats and pest suppression (e.g. maintaining forest patches, hedgerows, and other woody elements), their implementation has not been systematically monitored and evaluated within an adaptive management framework.
column36: Isolated or pilot implementations exist on a limited scale, often within research projects only.
column37: Implementation of bat-supportive management in Hungarian vineyards appears to be limited and largely indirect. Landscape features known to benefit bats, such as forest patches, hedgerows, and other woody habitats, occur in some vineyard landscapes, but they are generally not managed specifically for bat conservation. While research has identified habitat connectivity and proximity to forests as important factors promoting bat activity and pest suppression, there is little evidence that these recommendations have been widely adopted as targeted management practices across the vineyard sector. Current implementation is therefore best characterized as local and limited rather than widespread.

column30: Some ideas or untested strategies (e.g., installing artificial roosts) exist but have not been adapted to this system or tested rigorously.
column33: Irregular monitoring or anecdotal evidence to support their effectiveness, but data are not consistently or formally used to adapt or improve management strategies.
column36: No implementation of bat-supportive strategies occurs anywhere in the system.

column17: Mixed design with both organic and conventional
column20: No. Olive crops are clustered around a small massif (Montgrí), the sorrounding plains (>90% of the area) are cultivated with annual irrigated crops (mainly maize) and other perennial crops (mainly apple)
column21: Bats known to consume pests or overlap in activity with pests (indirect evidence of bat benefits).
column22: Olive fruit fly (Bactrocera oleae) is a major pest in the olive groves of the region, and a negative relation between its density and the presence of gleaning bats was found (Puig-Montserrat et al. 2021). No dietary analysis were conducted to further provide evidences of biological control.
column24: From a few sites or seasons - data collected from more than one farm or site, or during limited crop seasons (1 - 3 years). The representativeness of the findings to the whole system is limited.
column25: We collected data from 10 different blocks belonging to different cultivation plots within the region. Each block containing a conventinal olive grove, an organic olive grove, and a sparse conifer stand used as control. Survey during two seasons (years).
column27: Anecdotal evidence, informal observations, or studies from this crop elsewhere, suggest some possible threats (e.g., habitat loss), but without systematic investigation or confirmation in this specific system.
column28: The bat community of the massif where the olive groves are found has been extensively studied. We know from previous radiotrackings that some gleaning bats that breed in the massif are confined to the foothill, since the rest of the sorrounding habitats are too degradated (Flaquer et al 2008).
column30: Some ideas or untested strategies (e.g., installing artificial roosts) exist but have not been adapted to this system or tested rigorously.
column31: An important roost for cave dwellers has been managed and preserved in the area, with populations known to feed on the olive groves (see Flaquer et al 2008)
column33: Irregular monitoring or anecdotal evidence to support their effectiveness, but data are not consistently or formally used to adapt or improve management strategies.
column36: Isolated or pilot implementations exist on a limited scale, often within research projects only.

column20: Yes, rice is the major cultivation, and some other irrigated annual cultures exist in the area (mainly maize)
column21: Bats known to consume pests or overlap in activity with pests (indirect evidence of bat benefits).
column22: Both non-biting midges (Chironomidae), which constitute a major crop pest, and mosquitoes (Culicidae) were found in the diet of bats around rice paddies. Moreover bats actively exploited the emergence of adult mosquitoes with higher bat activity correlated to mosquitoe density  (Puig-Montserrat et al 2020).
column24: From a few sites or seasons - data collected from more than one farm or site, or during limited crop seasons (1 - 3 years). The representativeness of the findings to the whole system is limited.
column25: We collected data from 15 sampling locations, belonging to different cultivation plots, within the region. Survey during a single season.
column27: Anecdotal evidence, informal observations, or studies from this crop elsewhere, suggest some possible threats (e.g., habitat loss), but without systematic investigation or confirmation in this specific system.
column28: Important parts of the rice paddies are continuous monocultures with no roosts availability, or alternative habitats to ensure other sources of food for the bas.
column30: Some ideas or untested strategies (e.g., installing artificial roosts) exist but have not been adapted to this system or tested rigorously.
column31: An extensive network of bat boxes had been placed in the area
column33: Irregular monitoring or anecdotal evidence to support their effectiveness, but data are not consistently or formally used to adapt or improve management strategies.
column36: Widespread and sustained implementation of bat-supportive management practices occurs across the majority of relevant farms in the region.
column37: Rice producers of the region are very aware of the potential biological control bats exert, mainly on the rice borer moth (Chilo suppressalis) after the positive results obtained in the Ebro Delta (see Puig-Montserrat et al 2015). Accordingly, in most rice growing areas bat boxes are deployed by farmers.

column20: Yes, in both areas the vineyard dominates the land use.
column21: Direct evidence of pest suppression by bats (e.g. exclusion studies, reductions in pest damage where bats are present).
column22: Two exclosures were deployed in different areas with varying densities of European grapevine moth (Lobesia botrana). In one of them the pest damage was very low, in the other (Girona) the damage was economically significnat, and a 59% higher in exclosure than in the control plot.
column24: From a few sites or seasons - data collected from more than one farm or site, or during limited crop seasons (1 - 3 years). The representativeness of the findings to the whole system is limited.
column25: Two sampling locations placed at >150km of distance. Survey during a single season.
column27: No research or information on threats or limiting factors exists in this specific study system.
column28: Both vineyards are sorrounded with diverse mosaics that provide both shelter and food for the bats.
column30: Some ideas or untested strategies (e.g., installing artificial roosts) exist but have not been adapted to this system or tested rigorously.
column31: Some bat boxes had been deployed in both regions next to the experimental plots
column33: Monitoring is in place but limited in spatial or temporal scale, and data are not consistently or formally used to adapt or improve management strategies.
column36: Moderate adoption exists across some farms or stakeholder groups, but implementation is uneven or not widespread.
column37: Extensive dissemination efforts have been conducted in the region to transfer the current knowledge on the regulatory ecosystem services bats provide. Most wine producers are aware of it and bat boxes are often found around vineyards.
